next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 16

The conference room windows looked out on the loading dock, where a gray skin of old ice had survived three days of weak sun by hiding in the strip of shadow thrown by the warehouse wall. Nate stood at the end of the table with a legal pad he did not need and watched Ray flatten the Eastbridge site plan with both palms. The paper had been opened and rolled and opened again so many times the corners no longer obeyed. Somebody had drawn a thick orange perimeter around the whole footprint months ago. This morning someone else had added block letters in blue marker across the center: DEMO 12/28. There were twelve days left.

Not the barbershop. Not Gail's counter. Not Unit 7. Just demo, as if the whole strip mall had already become one thing.

Ray sat down first. He had a dry split at the edge of his thumb from the weather and kept catching it against the spiral on his notebook. He swore under his breath once, put the thumb in his mouth without thinking, then went back to flipping through a packet of permits. He looked tired in the stale fluorescent way everybody in the office looked tired by December, necks disappearing into quarter-zips, faces roughened by radiator heat and the dark at five o'clock.

Victor came in last with his coat over one arm and a clementine in the other hand. He'd peeled it cleanly, one orange ribbon dangling from his fingers. The room picked up the smell before he sat down.

"All right," he said. "Let's get this out of the way."

He did not sound grim. He sounded mildly inconvenienced, the way he sounded discussing snow removal or a delay...
open_hooks_to_carry: Tommy at 11 PM: split lip, shaking, the four-thousand-dollar number
The kitchen negotiation: Tommy's desperate optimism, Nate's refusal to enable, the reality of the checking account
prev_tail_excerpt:
...in the diner window. Lazar's Hardware was still lit. Through the glass Nate could see Mort in his canvas apron, head bent over the counter, sorting something small into little cardboard boxes with the absorbed patience of a man doing the task in front of him because the larger one could not be touched tonight.

Screws, probably. Washers. Left-hand thread, maybe. The kind nobody else stocked.

Nate stayed in the car with the engine running until the windshield fogged at the edges. He had promised to look into it. He had looked. This was what there was: investors, councilmen, brother-in-laws, handshakes, schedules, a boss who called himself a cog and meant it. Dominic had seen it. Dominic had tried. Dominic had failed and then kept working because the machine did not care whether the man feeding it believed in it.

Mort straightened inside the store and rubbed at the small of his back with one hand. Then he went back to sorting.

Nate did not go in. He sat with both hands on the steering wheel, Dominic's broad scarred hands at ten and two like he was learning to drive again, and understood at last the exact trap Dominic had lived in. It was not one bad decision. It was a series of ordinary continuations. Keep the job. Keep the apartment. Keep the people who depend on you from feeling the ground shift. Sign the letter. Send the notice. Tell yourself you did what room allowed. By the time you noticed what you had become, the paperwork was already in the mail.

The rain ticked softly on the hood. Inside the store Mort slid another box across the counter and reached for the next.
state_deltas_required: Tommy's gambling debts producing physical danger, requiring Nate to spend money he cannot afford on obligations that belong to a dead man's family

Nate experiences the full weight of Dominic's family responsibilities. The financial cushion is destroyed. A moment of genuine brotherhood passes between Nate and Tommy — inherited, undeserved, and real.
